items.q: Is the tallest peak in a mass spectrum the molecular ion?
items.correct: Not necessarily — the tallest (base) peak is just the most abundant ion; the molecular ion is the highest m/z peak and is often small.
items.misconception: The molecular ion is always the biggest peak in the spectrum.
items.random_wrong: The molecular ion always appears at exactly m/z = 100.
items.why: Students conflate 'most important' with 'most intense'; many molecular ions fragment so readily that M⁺ is tiny.

items.q: Does an IR spectrum identify a whole molecule on its own?
items.correct: It mainly identifies functional groups from bond vibrations; confirming the full structure usually needs the fingerprint region or other techniques like NMR and mass spectrometry.
items.misconception: Each molecule shows one single IR peak that names it directly.
items.random_wrong: IR spectroscopy works by breaking the bonds and weighing the fragments.
items.why: Students expect a one-peak-per-compound lookup; in reality each bond type absorbs, and the pattern must be interpreted.

items.q: In ¹H NMR, does the number of signals equal the number of hydrogen atoms?
items.correct: No — the number of signals equals the number of distinct hydrogen environments; equivalent hydrogens contribute to one signal.
items.misconception: A molecule with six hydrogens always shows six NMR signals.
items.random_wrong: The number of NMR signals equals the number of carbon atoms.
items.why: Students count atoms rather than environments; ethane's six equivalent hydrogens give a single peak.

items.q: In a mass spectrum, the molecular ion peak gives:
items.why: Correct answer: The relative molecular mass. “The most stable fragment” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A very broad IR absorption from 2500–3300 cm⁻¹ together with a strong C=O peak suggests:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: A carboxylic acid. “An alkane” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A fragment peak 15 units below the molecular ion usually shows loss of:
items.why: It's CH₃. “OH” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a chromatogram a spot moves 2.4 cm while the solvent front moves 8.0 cm. Calculate the Rf value.
items.why: Exact answer: 0.3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
